title: Conroe ISD Board Meeting — March 17, 2026

items.title: Receive Information Regarding District-Wide Intruder Detection Audit Report
items.context: The board will receive a confidential briefing on the results of a district-wide audit examining how well the district can detect unauthorized intruders on its campuses. This is discussed in closed session under Texas law because it involves security measures and sensitive security information. The findings could inform future safety investments and procedural changes.

items.title: Legal Update
items.context: The board will receive a private legal briefing from the district's attorney. This may cover pending or anticipated litigation, legal risks, or other matters requiring attorney-client privilege. No public details are provided for this item.

items.title: Conduct Level III Grievance Hearing
items.context: The board will privately hear a formal grievance that has been escalated to the highest level of the district's complaint process (Level III). This involves a personnel matter and is heard in closed session to protect the privacy of the individuals involved.

items.title: Discuss Principal Candidates
items.context: The board will privately discuss candidates for multiple principal positions across the district. Personnel discussions are held in closed session as allowed by Texas law. The corresponding public votes on these appointments are listed under Section VII of the agenda.

items.title: Consideration and Possible Action on Level III Grievance
items.context: Following the closed-session grievance hearing, the board may take public action on the grievance outcome. All final decisions must be made in open session under Texas law. This could include upholding, modifying, or overturning a previous administrative decision related to the grievance.

items.title: 2026 USA Co-ed Cheerleading National Champions — Caney Creek High School
items.context: The board will publicly recognize Caney Creek High School's cheerleading team for winning the 2026 USA Co-ed Cheerleading National Championship. This is a celebratory recognition item with no action required.

items.title: 2026 NCA Game Day Cheerleading National Champions — College Park High School
items.context: The board will recognize College Park High School's cheerleading team for winning the 2026 NCA Game Day Cheerleading National Championship. This is a celebratory recognition item.

items.title: 2026 UIL Girls Wrestling State Champion — The Woodlands High School
items.context: The board will recognize a student-athlete from The Woodlands High School for winning a 2026 UIL Girls Wrestling state championship. This is a celebratory recognition item.

items.title: 2026 UIL Boys and Girls Wrestling State Champions — College Park High School
items.context: The board will recognize College Park High School wrestlers who won 2026 UIL state championships in both boys and girls wrestling. This is a celebratory recognition item.

items.title: 2026 UIL Boys Freestyle Swimming State Champion — Oak Ridge High School
items.context: The board will recognize a student-athlete from Oak Ridge High School for winning the 2026 UIL Boys Freestyle Swimming state championship. This is a celebratory recognition item.

items.title: 2026 UIL Girls Team Swimming State Champions — The Woodlands High School
items.context: The board will recognize The Woodlands High School girls swimming team for winning the 2026 UIL state team championship. This is a celebratory recognition item.

items.title: Consider and Approve Minutes
items.context: The board will review and approve the official minutes from a previous board meeting. This is a standard record-keeping action to ensure accurate documentation of prior board proceedings.

items.title: Consider and Approve Amendment to the Budget
items.context: The board will consider approving changes to the district's current budget. Budget amendments are common during the school year to reflect revised revenue projections, new expenditures, or reallocation of funds. **This item involves a financial decision that may affect district spending.**

items.title: Receive Human Resources Report and Consider and Approve Employment of Professional Personnel
items.context: The board will receive a report on staffing activity and vote to approve the hiring of professional staff (such as teachers, counselors, or administrators). This is a routine item that formalizes personnel decisions recommended by administration. **This is a personnel action item.**

items.title: Receive Financial Reports
items.context: The board will receive regular financial reports detailing the district's revenues, expenditures, and overall fiscal status. This is a standard transparency and oversight item — no vote is required, but the reports become part of the public record.

items.title: Consider and Approve Texas Teacher Evaluation and Support System (T-TESS) Appraisers
items.context: The board will approve individuals authorized to conduct formal teacher evaluations under the state's T-TESS framework. Texas law requires the board to designate certified appraisers who observe and evaluate teacher performance. This is a routine annual or periodic approval.

items.title: Request Ratification of the District's 2026–2027 Certification of Provision of Instructional Materials Form
items.context: The board will ratify a state-required certification confirming that the district will provide instructional materials (such as textbooks and digital resources) to students for the 2026–2027 school year. This is a compliance requirement under Texas education law.

items.title: Consider Authorizing the District to Enter into an Interlocal Agreement with Region 16 Education Service Center and Interlocal Agreement with ESC 16's Sponsored Cooperative TexBuy
items.context: The board will consider joining a cooperative purchasing arrangement through Region 16 Education Service Center and its TexBuy program. Interlocal agreements like this allow school districts to pool purchasing power with other government entities to get better pricing on goods and services. **This is a procurement/spending-related item.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Bradley Elementary
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at Bradley Elementary. This follows the closed-session discussion of principal candidates earlier in the meeting. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Suchma Elementary
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at Suchma Elementary. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Cox Intermediate
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at Cox Intermediate. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Travis Intermediate School
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at Travis Intermediate School. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Stockton Junior High School
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at Stockton Junior High School. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for York Junior High School
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at York Junior High School. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.**

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Approve Principal for Grand Oaks High School 9th
items.context: The board will vote on a recommended candidate for the principal position at the Grand Oaks High School 9th-grade campus. **This is a significant personnel decision affecting campus leadership.** The number of principal appointments on this agenda (seven total) suggests a notable period of leadership transition across the district.

items.title: Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary
items.context: The board will consider and vote on a mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary, which appears to be a new or recently renamed campus. Mascot selection is typically a community-involved process and the board provides final approval. **This is a policy/identity decision for the campus.**

items.title: Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Lynn Cartwright Junior High School
items.context: The board will consider and vote on a mascot for Lynn Cartwright Junior High School, which also appears to be a new or recently renamed campus. As with the item above, this formalizes a campus identity decision.

items.title: Receive District and Campus Future Enrollment Projection Information from Zonda Demographics
items.context: The board will receive a presentation from Zonda Demographics (a demographic research firm) about projected future student enrollment at the district and campus levels. Enrollment projections are critical for planning facility needs, staffing, budgets, and attendance boundaries. This is an informational item — no vote is expected.

items.title: Discuss and Possibly Consider Local School Library Advisory Council (LSLAC)
items.context: The board will discuss the Local School Library Advisory Council, which is a body that can provide input on library-related policies and materials. Under recent Texas legislation (Senate Bill 13), districts may establish or utilize such councils in connection with the review of library materials. The board may take action or simply discuss the council's role and composition.

items.title: Discussion of Commemorative Months and Other Issues Under School Board Policy EMB
items.context: The board will discuss how the district observes or recognizes commemorative months (such as Black History Month, Hispanic Heritage Month, etc.) and related matters under Board Policy EMB. This could involve reviewing current practices, considering changes, or clarifying expectations around school-based observances. **This is a policy discussion item that may generate community interest.**

items.title: Consider and Possibly Approve Library Materials in Accordance with Texas Education Code §33.026, School Board Policy EFB, and Senate Bill 13
items.context: The board will vote on whether to approve specific library materials for district school libraries. Under Senate Bill 13 and related state law, school boards are required to review and approve certain library materials, particularly those that have been flagged or reviewed through a formal process. **This is a policy action item that may attract community attention, as library material decisions have been a topic of significant public interest statewide.**

items.title: Receive Information Regarding the Beginning and Middle of Year Northwest Evaluation Association Measures of Academic Progress Growth and Reading Fluency Assessment, House Bill 3 Progress Measure 1.2 and 2.2, and Bluebonnet Learning ELAR K–5
items.context: The board will receive a data presentation on student academic performance, including results from NWEA MAP Growth assessments (a nationally normed test measuring student growth), reading fluency assessments, progress toward House Bill 3 early literacy goals, and Bluebonnet Learning's English Language Arts and Reading (ELAR) program for grades K–5. This is an informational item to help the board and public understand how students are progressing academically during the current school year.

items.title: Receive Personnel Audit Presentation by the Docentus Group
items.context: The board will receive a presentation from the Docentus Group, an external consulting firm, regarding a personnel audit of the district. A personnel audit typically examines staffing levels, organizational structure, efficiency, and alignment of human resources with district needs. **This item could have implications for future staffing decisions and budget planning.**

items.title: Consider and Approve the Guaranteed Maximum Price Amendment for the Irons, Peet, York, and Conroe High School 9th Renovations Project and Authorize the Superintendent to Complete Negotiations and Execute the Contract Documents
items.context: The board will vote on a Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) amendment for renovation projects at Irons and Peet Elementary Schools, York Junior High School, and the Conroe High School 9th-grade campus. A GMP sets the maximum amount the district will pay a contractor for the work, protecting the district from cost overruns. The board would also authorize the Superintendent to finalize and sign the contracts. **This is a significant spending item involving construction contracts for multiple campuses.**

items.title: Consider and Accept as Complete the Armstrong, Austin, Birnham Woods, and Broadway Elementary Renovations Project
items.context: The board will vote to formally accept the renovation work at Armstrong, Austin, Birnham Woods, and Broadway Elementary Schools as complete. This is a standard step in the construction process that formally closes out a project, triggers final payments to the contractor, and begins warranty periods.

items.title: Consider and Accept as Complete Conroe High School Tennis Courts at the 9th Grade Campus Project
items.context: The board will vote to formally accept the construction of tennis courts at the Conroe High School 9th-grade campus as complete. As with the item above, this is a standard project closeout action.

# Conroe ISD Board of Trustees — Regular Meeting Breakdown

**Date:** Tuesday, March 17, 2026, 5:00 PM
**Location:** Walter P. Jett Continuing Education Center, 19403 David Memorial Drive, Shenandoah, TX 77385
**Virtual Access:** [http://tiny.conroeisd.net/R78KV](http://tiny.conroeisd.net/R78KV)

---

## Procedural Items

- **I.A.** Invocation
- **I.B.** Pledge of Allegiance
- **V.** Citizen Participation
- **XII.** Take Requests from Trustees Regarding Future Board Agenda Items
- **XIII.** Adjourn

---

## Closed Session

### II.A.1. Receive Information Regarding District-Wide Intruder Detection Audit Report

**Context:** The board will receive a confidential briefing on the results of a district-wide audit examining how well the district can detect unauthorized intruders on its campuses. This is discussed in closed session under Texas law because it involves security measures and sensitive security information. The findings could inform future safety investments and procedural changes.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet.

---

### II.B.1. Legal Update

**Context:** The board will receive a private legal briefing from the district's attorney. This may cover pending or anticipated litigation, legal risks, or other matters requiring attorney-client privilege. No public details are provided for this item.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet.

---

### II.C.1. Conduct Level III Grievance Hearing

**Context:** The board will privately hear a formal grievance that has been escalated to the highest level of the district's complaint process (Level III). This involves a personnel matter and is heard in closed session to protect the privacy of the individuals involved.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet.

---

**Context:** The board will privately discuss candidates for multiple principal positions across the district. Personnel discussions are held in closed session as allowed by Texas law. The corresponding public votes on these appointments are listed under Section VII of the agenda.

**Historical Summary:** No prior history available yet.

**Context:** Following the closed-session grievance hearing, the board may take public action on the grievance outcome. All final decisions must be made in open session under Texas law. ## Consent Agenda

## Administration

### VIII.A. Receive Information and Approve the Campus Mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary

**Context:** The board will consider and vote on a mascot for Kacy Arnold Elementary, which appears to be a new or recently renamed campus. Mascot selection is typically a community-involved process and the board provides final approval. **This is a policy/identity decision for the campus.**

---

### VIII.C. Receive District and Campus Future Enrollment Projection Information from Zonda Demographics

**Context:** The board will receive a presentation from Zonda Demographics (a demographic research firm) about projected future student enrollment at the district and campus levels. Enrollment projections are critical for planning facility needs, staffing, budgets, and attendance boundaries. This is an informational item — no vote is expected.

**Context:** The board will discuss the Local School Library Advisory Council, which is a body that can provide input on library-related policies and materials. Under recent Texas legislation (Senate Bill 13), districts may establish or utilize such councils in connection with the review of library materials. The board may take action or simply discuss the council's role and composition.

**Context:** The board will vote on whether to approve specific library materials for district school libraries. meeting_notes_md:
# Conroe ISD Board Meeting — 2026-03-17
*Regular Meeting — Unofficial Notes*
*Based on auto-generated transcript. Not an official record.*
*Generated 2026-03-25 19:43 UTC*

---

# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe Independent School District Board of Trustees
### Regular Meeting — March 17, 2026

> **Note:** These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript and may contain errors or omissions. For official minutes, please consult the district's records.

---

## Meeting Overview

- **Called to Order:** 5:02 PM by Board President Odin Weller
- **Adjourned:** 10:56 PM
- **Quorum Present:** Yes (all votes recorded as 7-0)
- **Location:** Not at the usual administration office; future meetings will return to the admin office temporarily due to end-of-year student events

**Procedural Changes Announced:** President Odin Weller outlined several new meeting structure changes:
1. **Closed session moved to 5:00 PM** (before the 6:00 PM public session) to avoid making the audience wait
2. **Special recognitions modified** — certificates presented before the meeting in a reception; honorees still acknowledged publicly
3. **Principal/leadership appointments** now handled through the consent agenda rather than individual votes
4. These changes are intended to streamline meetings given the district's size (~$760 million budget) and the board meeting only once per month

---

## Closed Session

- **Entered:** 5:08 PM
- **Returned to Open Session:** 6:31 PM
- **Sections Cited:** Texas Government Code §§ 551.076, 551.071, 551.074

### Action Taken:
**Motion:** To uphold the Level 1 and Level 2 grievance decision and authorize the board president to issue a written determination.
- **Vote: 7-0, Approved**

---

## Special Recognitions

### Caney Creek High School Co-Ed Cheer — 2026 USA National Champions
- Competed in Anaheim, CA at the USA Spirit National Championships (co-ed division)
- Scored 98.6 in finals; also earned silver in best overall fight song
- Head Coach: Jaylen Jackson; Assistants: Noazi, Kathy Peara, Peggy Lions
- Principal Dr. Jeff Stickler present

### College Park High School Cheer — 2026 UCA Division 1 National Champions
- Competed in Orlando, FL in the UCA Division 1 National High School Championships (super large game day)
- Earned automatic placement in finals after Day 1; won national championship
- Coaches: Abby Constantino, Maggie Meadow, Chandler Howard
- Principal Robert McFarland present
- President Odin Weller noted both cheer programs have supported each other across schools

### Claire Giden, The Woodlands High School — 2026 UIL Girls Wrestling State Champion
- 155-pound junior; won all matches (except district) by pin or technical fall
- Coach: William Durning
- Principal Dennis Mesler present

### College Park High School Wrestling — 2026 UIL State Champions
- District champions 8th year in a row; dual state champions 2nd year in a row; 2nd place team at state
- **Individual State Champions:**
  - **Mason Milsaps** (132 lbs, freshman) — 40-4 record; two-time US Open national champion; Pan-American champion for Team USA
  - **Landry von Gotten** (135 lbs, junior) — 42-1 record; third state championship; multi-time national champion; will represent Team USA at Pan-American Games
  - **Logan Milsaps** (144 lbs, senior) — 38-3 record; two-time state champion; team captain; 4.3 GPA; will wrestle for Arkansas Little Rock
- Coaches: Eric Sput, Lindsay Spute, Kenneth Mark, Brad Ewing

### Micah Tennyson, Oak Ridge High School — 2026 UIL Freestyle Swimming State Champion
- Junior; won both 100 and 200 freestyle at state
- Holds school records in 100, 200, and 500 freestyle
- Head Coach: Christine Ross; Assistant: Jeff Spoon
- Principal Nina Norman present

### The Woodlands High School Girls Swimming — 2026 UIL Team State Champions
- Won their **13th state title**; clinched the championship before the final event
- Coaches: Jeremy Wade, Kevin Wit, Marty Miller, Dylan Venter
- **Special Announcement:** Brooke Miller named TGCA Swimming Athlete of the Year

---

## Citizen Participation (Public Comment)

### Palin Lackey (Grand Oaks High School freshman)
- Advocated for **female flag football to become a UIL sport**
- Cited safety statistics for female athletes in tackle football (176,831 ER visits over 10 years)
- Noted Houston Texans and Dallas Cowboys are willing to sponsor new teams/clubs
- Requested formal district support to petition the state for UIL approval

### Mark Brooks
- Thanked the board for discussing library material reviews in open session last month
- Raised concerns about the review process:
  - Challenge forms were not released publicly; he obtained them through a public information request
  - The justification on one challenge form (for *Ann of West Philly*) referenced content including "lesbians getting drunk and puking" that was not discussed in open session
  - Reviews appear to be based on "feelings" rather than applying **local policy EFB**
  - Quoted EFB policy criteria including advice of superintendent, grade-level suitability, library standards, and academic expert reviews
- **Requested the board vote to establish a library advisory council at the next meeting**

### Kristen Brown (Oak Ridge feeder zone parent)
- Supported thoughtful discussion of book challenges
- Noted the **board packet was not available until approximately 3:00 PM** on meeting day (590 pages); insufficient time for public review
- In January, it wasn't posted before the meeting started
- Encouraged timely public posting of board packets

### Michelle Taylor (Retired CISD Homebound math teacher, 21 years)
- Referenced neuroscientist Dr. Jared Horath's testimony before the US Senate about Generation Z being the first generation not to outscore the previous one on cognitive testing, attributed to overuse of technology
- Noted Sweden is spending hundreds of millions to return to textbooks
- Shared personal example of a 10-year-old grandson with dysgraphia, ADHD, and anxiety struggling with computer-based testing, especially in math
- **Requested the district reduce computer-based testing and return to textbooks and paper tests, starting with math**

---

## Consent Agenda (Items A–J)

- **Vote: 7-0, Approved** as presented (no items pulled for separate consideration)
- Included the Human Resources report (which encompassed all principal appointments)

---

## Human Resources — Principal Appointments

*All appointments were approved as part of the consent agenda. Individual presentations were made for recognition purposes.*

## Administration

### A. Casey Arnold Elementary Mascot
- School colors: Navy blue and green
- Community voted between owls and panthers
- **Owls selected** — noted as meaningful because owls represent reading and education, honoring Casey Arnold's legacy
- **Motion to approve the owl as official mascot**
- **Vote: 7-0, Approved**

### B. Lynn Cartwright Junior High Mascot
- Four nominees from parents/students: Chargers, Colts, Mustangs, Cougars
- **Cougars selected** overwhelmingly by community vote
- Professional mascot rendering to be developed by outside vendor
- **Motion to approve Cougars as official mascot**
- **Vote: 7-0, Approved**

### C. District and Campus Future Enrollment Projections (Zonda Demographics)

**Presenter:** Rocky (Zonda Demographics) — first quarterly report under new contract

**Key Findings:**
- CISD closed 3,335 homes last year; ranked by closings (occupied homes)
- Housing starts declining; ~500 homes started last quarter (one of the smallest start quarters)
- Closings expected to fall below 3,000 next year (possibly as low as 2,800)
- ~1,200 homes under construction; ~600-700 finished but vacant
- Interest rates and affordability cited as factors in slowdown
- 88 active subdivisions; 5,350 lots ready to build; 22,000 future lots tracked

**Enrollment Data:**
- **First enrollment decline in 10+ years** — district lost ~150 students last year
- Kindergarten class declined by 200 students
- Cohort growth (students staying grade-to-grade) remains strong at ~1.0 or above — once students enroll, they tend to stay
- School of choice and homeschooling identified as factors in kindergarten enrollment decline
- Leaver/newcomer differential less than 200 students
- Hispanic student population stable locally; statewide decline of 60,000 Hispanic students noted
- 60% of builders now buying down interest rates to help families

**Campus Capacity Concerns:**
- **Bartlett Elementary:** Projected to grow significantly (~600 students); identified as a real challenge
- **San Jacinto Elementary:** Small school (700 capacity) approaching nearly 200% capacity; potentially more than doubling in 10 years
- **Austin, Patterson, Stewart:** Being monitored; portables may be needed to bridge gaps
- District owns land at Carter Ranch, Marvilla, east of Gordon Reed Elementary, Grand Oaks layaway space, and Evergreen (Campbell) for future construction

**Projections:**
- District projected to stabilize around 71,500-72,000 students in 10 years
- High and low scenarios presented; low scenario shows potential loss of 400+ students if current trends continue
- Total district projected to be ~4,000 students under total capacity by 2035

**District Response:**
- Demographic data already used for staffing this year, **saving $6.3 million**
- Quarterly reports will be provided going forward
- Master facilities plan development beginning (expected May/June)
- Next quarterly report will include mobility data (where transfers are going, charter school enrollment)
- Portables being added at Patterson and San Jacinto as bridge solutions

**Significant Board Discussion:**
- President Odin Weller noted Townsen Bridge development (in Arnold zone) has significant flooding concerns; discussions ongoing with county commissioners and state representatives about potentially purchasing land for flood reserves
- Trustee asked about controllable factors for student retention; Dr. Vincent discussed virtual learning expansion as a future strategy (cited Hallsville ISD with 2,000 virtual students in a 4,000-student district)
- Discussion about optimal campus capacity percentage — referenced 90% as when a school "feels full"; varies by design and grade level
- Dr. Vincent emphasized this is "step one" toward a master facilities plan and that the board will be asked about their vision and guardrails

## Teaching and Learning

### A. Library Materials (Texas Education Code 33026, Policy EFB, SB 13) — **TABLED**
- Administration requested tabling to allow implementation of newly purchased software for thorough review of materials
- Dr. Vincent asked for patience; emphasized the importance of getting it right
- Trustee expressed appreciation for efforts; noted it is "very important" to the board
- Dr. Vincent acknowledged it is both law and policy

### B. Academic Progress Presentation (Information Only)

*Comprehensive presentation covering NWA MAP assessment, Blue Bonnet Learning curriculum, House Bill 3 progress measures, and reading fluency*

#### Blue Bonnet Learning Curriculum Survey Results

**Presenter:** Darren Carile (and team)

**Surveys conducted:** Fall (October) and Winter (end of February) — Teachers, school leaders, and parents surveyed

**Glows (Positives):**
- Strong parent engagement: 1,500+ parents in fall survey; 934 in winter survey
- Teachers comfortable with Blue Bonnet web portal resources
- Over 50% of parents receiving unit parent letters
- Phonics instruction highly praised, especially in foundational grades
- Professional development from elementary language arts team praised as "first class"
- Optional virtual "coffee chats" for teacher support well-received

**Grows (Areas for Improvement):**
- **Text complexity** perceived as high for grade level — addressed as intentional per science of reading (listening comprehension vs. reading comprehension require different skills; complex text used during teacher-led read-alouds)
- **Pacing** — teachers struggling to fit lessons in time frames (improving but still a concern); curriculum designed for 180 days but average instruction days are fewer
- **Bilingual/ESL support** — built-in scaffolds insufficient; teachers creating additional supports
- **STAAR alignment** concerns from parents and teachers (though unit assessments and STAAR scores are showing alignment)
- **Remediation and acceleration** resources — a gap exists due to no reading interventions making the HQIM list from the State Board of Education
- Teachers prefer more **student-led** vs. teacher-led instruction (addressed as inherent to the direct instruction model)
- Need for additional anchor charts, sentence stems for emergent bilingual and special needs students

#### NWA MAP Assessment Results

**Presenter:** Melinda Stewart

**Key Findings:**
- Computer-adaptive test administered 3 times per year (beginning, middle, end)
- **3-point jump** in English achievement from beginning to middle of year
- **4-point jump** in Spanish achievement from beginning to middle of year
- **Median growth achievement exceeds the national average**
- **52% of students** in top two quartiles (highest and high-average) at mid-year
- **37 of 40 campuses** above national growth projections
- **CISD district growth exceeds national growth**
- A fourth of CISD students reaching the highest quartile

#### Blue Bonnet Unit Assessment Results

**Presenter:** Mr. Smith

- ~10 unit assessments per grade level; electronic format; STAAR-aligned
- Added extended constructed response (essay) components based on teacher feedback
- Average scores by grade level track closely with STAAR performance:
  - Kindergarten: 59% (compared to 54% for 3rd grade STAAR)
  - Similar patterns across K-5
- Unit assessments are **not designed to predict STAAR scores** but show students are tracking in the right direction
- Assessments created by same team that designed the curriculum (unlike previous locally-created assessments)
- Spanish assessments being trans-adapted internally (not provided by the state); kudos to bilingual/ESL team

#### House Bill 3 Progress Measures

- **Early childhood literacy goal:** 55% of K-3 students reaching MAP growth projection by 2030
- **Current year goal of 51% achieved across all grade levels (K-3)**
- Goal is above the national norm
- 37 of 40 campuses above the national 51% average
- Progress measure is adaptive; board can re-examine targets

#### Strategic Direction — Drive to 90

**Dr. Vincent outlined the path to an A rating by 2027:**
- Curriculum audit underway with Carrie Gain (deputy director at Region 13)
- Areas of analysis: district accountability, campus data, CCMR, graduation rate, school improvement framework
- Comparison being made to top 10 largest Texas districts
- **Identified "low-hanging fruit":** STAAR performance, dropout rate coding, graduation rate optimization, English proficiency (TELPAS), CCMR coding improvements
- Dropout rate issues partly attributed to coding problems, not actual drops
- CCMR improvements possible through coding, strategic student scheduling, and incentives (e.g., no senior privileges until CCMR met)

**Principal/Leadership Experience Concern:**
- Average years of principal experience declined from ~8 years to ~4.5 years
- District focusing on hiring and developing instructional leaders

#### Future Organizational Structure

**Presenter:** Dr. Landry

- Current support systems described as "disjointed" with "no continuity" — a "desert of systems"
- **New structure planned:**
  - Single point of contact through Assistant Superintendent of Schools
  - Subject-area curriculum specialists by grade level (not generalists)
  - School improvement experts with vertical understanding for boots-on-the-ground campus support
  - Operations and finance support integrated
  - Dedicated professional development program for principals and associate principals
- **Elementary coaches being reallocated to interventionists** for direct Tier 2/Tier 3 student support
- **Middle school support** identified as critical need; dedicated expertise being developed for 5th-8th grade

#### Dosensus Group Input

**Presenter:** Ron Morrison

- Connected the academic presentation to their organizational review findings
- Emphasized three concepts: **Value, Voice, Vigor**
- Noted the #1 finding in their report was that staff know the mission, but "noise" (system inconsistency) is in the way
- Praised the shift from measurement tools to instructional drivers
- Noted teacher input being sought (voice) as critical
- Cautioned about balancing momentum with sustainability to reduce friction

**Significant Board Discussion:**
- Multiple trustees expressed strong support for Blue Bonnet and the science of reading approach
- Trustee asked about support for special education documentation in Blue Bonnet — guidance documents being developed to help special education teachers navigate the curriculum; suggestion made to push this recommendation to TEA
- Change fatigue acknowledged as a real concern; Dr. Vincent noted data supports fewer initiatives and allowing teachers to metabolize changes
- Dr. Vincent shared that at his previous district (Wylie ISD), every campus was rated A with every distinction
- **Blue Bonnet Texas Playbook** — district received a grant to create an implementation guide for other districts
- **Dr. Upshaw named to the 10-member Texas Council of Academic Officers** — providing CISD a seat at the table for state curriculum decisions
- Presentation will be made available online

## Business/Finance

### Personnel Audit Presentation (Dosensus Group) — Information Only

**Presenter:** Tiffany (Dosensus Group), reviewing the Mo Casey staffing study

**Key Findings:**

1. **Overall lean staffing:** CISD has higher student-to-staff ratios than peer districts (Cy-Fair, Fort Bend, Humble, Katy, Spring), regional, and state averages — indicating fewer staff per student
   - District ratio: 7.7 students per total staff vs. 7.5 peer average
   - Especially lean in **support staff, student support staff, and auxiliary staff**

2. **Central administration outlier — potential overstaffing:**
   - CISD has the **lowest** central administration student-to-staff ratio compared to peers (meaning more central admin per student)
   - Sharp decline in ratio coincided with TEA recoding instructional coaches into central administration (2022-23)
   - Prior to recoding, CISD had the **highest** ratio (leanest central admin); after recoding, dropped to the **lowest** (most central admin per student)
   - The graph showed a much more dramatic shift for CISD than the statewide/regional trend

3. **Experienced workforce:** Over two-thirds of teachers have 6+ years of experience — commended as a strength

4. **Middle/junior high schools lean:** Student-to-total-staff ratio of 12:1 vs. district overall of 7.7:1 — needs monitoring

5. **Funding:** Revenue relies more heavily on local property taxes than regional/state averages

**Five Recommendations:**

1. **Multi-phase staffing allocation plan** using new data, aligned to programmatic needs, instructional philosophy, and budget
2. **Staff reductions (if needed)** should rely heavily on **attrition and reassignment** with clear communication, kindness, and advanced notice
3. **Reorganize central administration** to address apparent overstaffing of instructional coaches and other inefficiencies
4. **2026-27:** If further reductions needed, conduct **programmatic reviews** using objective criteria; classroom ratio adjustments should be a **last step**
5. **Future staffing additions** should prioritize areas with highest student-to-staff ratios: student services, aides, auxiliary staff, and middle/junior high school overall staffing

**Sample Multi-Phase Plan Presented:**
- Phase 1: March–June 2026 (already underway)
- Phase 2: Summer 2026 through 2027-28 (semester-by-semester if further reductions needed)
- Underlying principles: honor staff, maximize attrition, provide clear communication, allow staff voice/choice, advance notice

**Dr. Vincent's Comments:**
- Already reallocated district-level coaches and secondary-level positions
- Elementary coaches being converted to interventionists
- District already nearly "in the black" due to cuts and adjustments
- Referenced that demographic data already saved **$6.3 million** through appropriate staffing
- Legislative advocacy needed for special education funding (federal/state fund only ~35% of actual needs)

## Planning and Construction

### A. GMP Amendment — Irons, Pete York, and Conroe High School 9th Renovation

- Construction manager: Angelo Bryant; Design team: VLK
- **Guaranteed Maximum Price: $10,095,300**
- Original construction target: $11,500,000 — came in under budget
- Work includes: chillers, boilers, freezer coolers, roof repairs, generators, electrical service gear, safety and security improvements (2023 bond)
- Savings returned to bond fund for other critical infrastructure projects
- **Vote: 7-0, Approved**

### B. Accept as Complete — Armstrong, Austin, Burnham Woods, and Broadway Project

- Critical infrastructure project; required board acceptance per policy CV Local (projects over $1 million)
- Financial audit completed internally; design team certified closeout documents
- **Returned $639,282.24 to the district** (finished under GMP)
- Funds return to bond codes for other projects
- **Vote: 7-0, Approved**

### C. Accept as Complete — Conroe High School Tennis Courts (9th Grade Campus)

- Courts completed January 2025; used for spring 2025 season
- CSP project; zero change orders
- **Final cost: $2,355,933**
- Closeout took 13-14 months due to disputed items with contractor (resolved to district's benefit)
- President Odin Weller clarified that questions asked about warranty documents and closeout procedures were part of the board's "trust but verify" approach to processes, not a reflection on Mr. Foster's team, given concerns about process gaps identified after previous administration changes
- **Vote: 7-0, Approved**
